docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
I am currently a tenured Professor in the Data Science and Anaytics （数据科学与分析学域）, and the Associate Dean of Information Hub （信息枢纽）, The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou), and I am also an affiliated Professor at HKUST. Previously, I was a Redbird Visiting Professor in the Department of Computer Science and Engineering, HKUST, and a Professor in the School of Computer Science and Engineering, University of New South Wales, Australia.

Contact

  • Email: weiwcs AT ust.hk
  • Office: W4 515 (Campus Map, Campus Navigator)
  • Google Scholar: https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=wLtu3FYAAAAJ&hl=en
  • Github: https://github.com/DBAIWangGroup

docs.title: Research
docs.text:
My research areas include Databases, Algorithms, Artificial Intelligence and Cross-disciplinary Topics. I have published research papers in these areas, with a majority of them in prestigious international journal and conferences.

I have won several awards, including SIGSPATIAL Best Vision Paper, ICASSP 2023 Top 3% Paper, SIGCOMM 2022 Best Paper, ICMR 2021 Best Paper and DASFAA 2016 Best Student Paper awards. In addition, several pieces of my research have been deployed in industrial projects and products.

My current research interests are:

  • Integration of database and artificial intelligence technologies, including learned indexes, learned algorithms and optimizations, and intelligent data preprocess and postprocessing (e.g., data cleansing/integration and NLP interface for DB/applications).
  • Machine Learning and Deep Learning, including Large Language Models, Theoretical Aspects of Machine/deep Learning, and Agentic AI.
  • Understanding high-dimensional data, including its structure, properties, and algorithms (e.g., vector databases).
  • Cross-disciplinary research and applications, including Material Informatics (e.g., material property prediction, material design, physics-informed neural networks, sybmolic regression, etc.), AI for Science (e.g., neural operator learning for solving PDEs), and AI for Education.

Also see

  • my Google Scholar page for a fairly up-to-date, manually curated list of publications --- my name is a challenge for Named Entity Disambiguation (e.g., "The base name with the most different numeric suffixes (i.e., identified authors) is Wei Wang" and motivated me to work on Entity Resolution
  • my GitHub page for a list of code repositories for my teaching and research.

docs.title: Teaching
docs.text:
• I am the Computer Science Coordinator of HKUST(GZ), in charge of all Computer Science-related courses in the first two years for our UG students.
  • I am overseeing UG teaching related issues in the Information Hub, HKUST(GZ).
  • Currently, we are building AI4Edu systems based on Large Language Models, with the aim to explore new learning paradigms.

docs.title: Service
docs.text:
I am an Associate Editor of IEEE TKDE, Journal of Material Informatics, and Data Science and Engineering. I also served as PC Co-chairs of IEEE BigData 2023 and APWeb 2012, PC Vice-chairs of ICDE 2014 and 2018. I also served in other organizational roles in many conferences and workshops, as well as reviewers and PC members for major journals and conferences.

I am the Vice Directors of Guangzhou Municipal Key Laboratory of Materials Informatics, Guangzhou Municipal Key Laboratory of Electronic Design Automation, Guangzhou Municipal Key Laboratory of Big Data and Intelligence.

docs.title: Publication Page Tag and Venue Filtering Design
Goal
docs.text:
Add client-side filtering to both publication pages so visitors can: - filter papers by a selected tag - filter papers by a selected venue - combine one tag and one venue with AND logic - clear the selection to restore all papers

This should work on: - docs/intro/publications.md - docs/intro/publications_selected.md

The feature must be generated by the publications pipeline so future rebuilds preserve it automatically.

docs.title: Filter controls
docs.text:
Each publication page will render a filter panel below the page title with: - a tag chip group - a venue chip group - a clear filters control - optional result count text

The user chose clickable chips rather than dropdowns.

docs.title: Interaction rules
docs.text:
• Clicking a tag chip selects that tag.
  • Clicking the selected tag chip again clears the tag selection.
  • Clicking a venue chip selects that venue.
  • Clicking the selected venue chip again clears the venue selection.
  • If both a tag and a venue are selected, only papers matching both remain visible.
  • Clicking clear removes both selections and shows all papers.
  • Year headings should automatically hide when every paper in that year has been filtered out.

docs.title: Progressive enhancement
docs.text: If JavaScript is unavailable, the full page should still render normally. Filtering is an enhancement, not a requirement for basic reading.

docs.title: Where to implement
docs.text:
Implement the feature in the shared publications rendering pipeline rather than editing generated pages manually.

Primary integration point: - tools/publications/build_publications.py

The actual rendering logic should generate the filter UI, per-entry metadata, and inline script for both publication pages.

docs.title: Page annotations
docs.text:
Each rendered publication entry should include normalized metadata via HTML data attributes, for example: - data-tags="llm db" - data-venue="icde" - data-year="2025"

Normalization should be stable and internal-only so visible labels can keep their current formatting.

docs.title: Filter UI generation
docs.text:
For each page, collect all unique: - tags from the records rendered on that page - venues from the records rendered on that page

Generate chip controls from those page-specific values so: - the selected-publications page shows only filters relevant to that page - the all-publications page shows filters relevant to the full dataset

docs.title: Client-side behavior
docs.text:
Embed a small JavaScript block that: 1. tracks the currently selected tag and venue 2. toggles active chip state 3. evaluates each paper entry against the selected filters 4. hides unmatched entries 5. recalculates visibility of year headers 6. updates clear/reset state and optional result count

Matching semantics: - no selection: show all - tag only: match papers containing that tag - venue only: match papers with that venue - both: match papers satisfying tag AND venue

docs.title: Styling
docs.text:
Extend docs/stylesheets/publications.css to style: - filter panel layout - clickable filter chips - active filter states - clear button - hidden entries and hidden year headers

The new UI should visually align with the existing publication chip styling and remain responsive on narrow screens.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Verify the following on both publication pages: - filter controls render correctly - tag filtering works - venue filtering works - combined tag + venue filtering uses AND logic - clear restores the full list - year headers disappear when all entries in that year are hidden - layout remains usable on desktop and mobile widths

docs.text:

docs.title: Publication Filters Implementation Plan
docs.text:
For Claude: REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task.

Goal: Add clickable tag and venue filters to both generated publication pages so visitors can filter by one tag, one venue, or both together, and clear filters to restore all papers.

Architecture: Extend the shared publications rendering pipeline so it emits filter controls, normalized data-* attributes on each publication entry, and a small inline client-side script. Keep the feature as progressive enhancement: without JavaScript the pages still render all publications normally, and with JavaScript the filters update entry and year-header visibility in place.

Tech Stack: Python publication generator, generated Markdown with embedded HTML/JS, MkDocs, pytest, shared CSS in docs/stylesheets/publications.css

docs.title: Task 2: Add normalization helpers and filter UI rendering
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: tools/publications/publications_lib.py - Test: tests/publications/test_publications_pipeline.py

Step 1: Write the failing test for normalization and page-specific options

Add or extend a test so it verifies: - venue labels are preserved for display - normalized values are lowercase slug-like strings for matching - selected page only includes tags/venues actually present on selected records

Example expectations:

assert 'data-value="vldb"' in selected_md
assert 'data-value="emnlp"' in all_md
assert 'ACL Findings' not in selected_md or only appears if a selected paper uses it

Step 2: Run test to verify it fails

Run: 

pytest tests/publications/test_publications_pipeline.py::test_build_publications_generates_cache_report_and_pages -v

Expected: FAIL because no normalization helpers or filter toolbar exist.

Step 3: Write minimal implementation

In tools/publications/publications_lib.py, add small helpers such as:

def normalize_filter_value(value: str) -> str:
    value = normalize_whitespace(value).casefold()
    value = re.sub(r"[^a-z0-9]+", "-", value).strip("-")
    return value


def render_filter_chip(group: str, label: str) -> str:
    value = normalize_filter_value(label)
    return (
        f'<button type="button" class="pub-filter-chip" '
        f'data-filter-group="{group}" data-value="{escape(value, quote=True)}">'
        f'{escape(label)}</button>'
    )

Add a helper to collect page-local unique tags and venues and render a top-of-page filter block.

Step 4: Run test to verify it passes

Run: 

pytest tests/publications/test_publications_pipeline.py::test_build_publications_generates_cache_report_and_pages -v

Expected: PASS for filter toolbar and normalized chip output.

Step 5: Commit

git add tools/publications/publications_lib.py tests/publications/test_publications_pipeline.py
git commit -m "feat: render publication filter controls"

docs.title: PhD
docs.text:
Several PhD positions with full scholarships are available. The PhD program at the Guangzhou campus has the same academic structure with the Clear Water Bay campus, including English as the teaching language, the same PhD graduate certificate, a highly competitive scholarship, etc.

HKUST(GZ) creates an exceptional opportunity for new resources, organizational structure and physical settings needed for the systematic development of cross-disciplinary programs in concert with the disciplinary-oriented efforts on the HKUST Clear Water Bay campus. Under the Unified HKUST-Complementary Campuses umbrella, academic structures and degree programs are differentiated, the overall infrastructures substantially enlarged and updated, and courses and central research facilities open to students and researchers both ways. Even though the two campuses are of separate legal entities and financial frameworks, faculty are encouraged and supported to collaborate and co-supervise students, and students will have opportunities to pursue degrees offered by both campuses. This way, members of both campuses will freely choose their research topics and related studies with University’s overall enlarged scope, with more flexibility and better support.

One can find more information about the PhD program at HKUST(GZ) here.

docs.title: VLDB20 - Similarity Query Processing
docs.text: This is the resource page for our VLDB 2020 tutorial titled "Similarity Query Processing for High-Dimensional Data" by Jianbin Qin, Wei Wang, Chuan Xiao, and Ying Zhang.

docs.title: Paper
docs.text: Jianbin Qin, Wei Wang, Chuan Xiao, Ying Zhang: Similarity Query Processing for High-Dimensional Data. Proc. VLDB Endow. 13(12): 3437-3440 (2020) pdf

docs.title: Abstract
docs.text:
Similarity query processing has been an active research topic for several decades. It is an essential procedure in a wide range of applications. Recently, embedding and auto-encoding methods as well as pre-trained models have gained popularity. They basically deal with high-dimensional data, and this trend brings new opportunities and challenges to similarity query processing for high-dimensional data. Meanwhile, new techniques have emerged to tackle this long-standing problem theoretically and empirically.

In this tutorial, we summarize existing solutions, especially recent advancements from both database (DB) and machine learning (ML) communities, and analyze their strengths and weaknesses. We review exact and approximate methods such as cover tree, locality sensitive hashing, product quantization, and proximity graphs. We also discuss the selectivity estimation problem and show how researchers are bringing in state-of-the-art ML techniques to address the problem. By highlighting the strong connections between DB and ML, we hope that this tutorial provides an impetus towards new ML for DB solutions and vice versa.
